name: Discrete Mathematics
description: Graph theory, combinatorics, formal logic, set theory, recursion, generating functions. The mathematics of countable, separable, finite objects. Foundation of computer science, network theory, formal verification, and combinatorial optimization. Depends on 444 (algebra via finite fields). Feeds into 666 (computation via automata and complexity), 699 (optimization via graph algorithms), and 700 (numerical methods via discrete approximation).
axioms: Pigeonhole principle: n items into m < n boxes → at least one box contains ≥ ⌈n/m⌉ items
Handshaking lemma: sum of vertex degrees = 2|E| in any graph
Principle of mathematical induction: P(0) ∧ (P(k) → P(k+1)) → ∀n P(n)
Graph duality: every planar graph has a dual graph
Kuratowski's theorem: a graph is planar iff it contains no K₅ or K₃,₃ minor
reasoning_patterns: combinatorial counting (permutations, combinations, stars and bars)
state machine modeling of discrete systems
graph coloring and network flow analysis
boundary_conditions: NP-complete problems (graph coloring, Hamiltonian path) have no known polynomial algorithm
Some combinatorial problems are #P-complete (counting variants even harder)
Graph isomorphism is in NP but not known to be NP-complete or polynomial
Ramsey numbers grow superexponentially — R(6,6) is unknown despite being a finite number
Many combinatorial structures are not classifiable
